opportunity_brief.goal: Turn a broad pricing topic into a concrete short-form breakdown
problem_statements: buyers do not understand why the premium tier exists
opportunity_brief.content_objective: Show how anchor tiers reduce price shock
opportunity_brief.freshness_rationale: SaaS teams are revisiting pricing in a tighter market
research_hypotheses: premium anchor tiers increase perceived plan value
success_criteria: viewer can explain why the middle plan feels safer

items.idea: Why most pricing pages hide the real reason enterprise exists
items.why_now: many teams are revisiting packaging in 2026
items.potential_hook: Your enterprise tier may only exist to make pro look cheap

items.idea: Use pricing anchors so your middle plan feels like the safe choice
items.problem: buyers default to the cheapest plan when value is unclear
items.why_now: budget pressure makes pricing clarity more important
items.potential_hook: If buyers jump to cheapest, your anchor tier is broken

rejection_reasons: one idea was too broad to prove

scores.reason: Best mix of clarity, proof, and distribution

scoring.selection_reasoning: The middle-plan framing is easier to prove and easier to execute in under 45 seconds.

angle_options.viewer_problem: buyers keep choosing the cheapest plan
angle_options.core_promise: Make your middle tier feel safer with a clear anchor plan
angle_options.primary_takeaway: A visible premium anchor clarifies the value of the middle plan
angle_options.cta: Follow for more SaaS teardown lessons
angle_options.why_this_version_should_exist: It turns an abstract pricing concept into a simple operator checklist

angles.selection_reasoning: Best fit for a short, proof-backed educational clip

audience_insights: Founders want clearer plan differentiation without adding discounting
competitor_observations: Many pricing pages explain features but not anchor logic
key_facts: Anchors shape perceived value before a buyer compares feature details
proof_points: Premium plans can make the middle tier feel safer instead of more expensive
examples: Compare a two-tier pricing page with a three-tier anchor setup
case_studies: Show how a premium decoy reframes the center plan
gaps_to_exploit: Competitors rarely explain why the top plan exists
research_pack.research_stop_reason: Enough evidence gathered for a short educational script

argument_map.thesis: The premium tier should frame the middle plan before buyers compare feature details.
argument_map.audience_belief_to_challenge: Better pricing copy is the first fix when buyers keep choosing cheapest.
argument_map.core_mechanism: Buyers anchor on plan contrast first and only then interpret feature differences through that frame.

proof_anchors.summary: Anchors shape perceived value before a buyer compares feature details.
proof_anchors.usage_note: Use to reframe why the top tier exists.

proof_anchors.summary: A premium decoy can make the middle plan feel safer instead of more expensive.
proof_anchors.usage_note: Use as the concrete example beat.

counterarguments.counterargument: Sophisticated buyers still compare features in detail.
counterarguments.response: They do, but only after the anchor has shaped which comparison feels sensible.

safe_claims.claim: Tier framing changes what buyers notice first.
A premium anchor can make the middle plan feel like the safer choice.

unsafe_claims.claim: A premium anchor always increases conversion.
unsafe_claims.note: Not supported as a universal fact.

beat_claim_plan.goal: Challenge the idea that the cheapest plan wins because your copy is weak.
beat_claim_plan.transition_note: Move from the false diagnosis into the anchor mechanism.

beat_claim_plan.goal: Explain that the premium tier exists to frame the middle option.
beat_claim_plan.transition_note: Set up one concrete pricing-page example.

beat_claim_plan.goal: Show the premium-anchor example and answer the obvious objection.
beat_claim_plan.transition_note: Turn the example into an operator rule.

beat_claim_plan.goal: Give the actionable audit rule and land the CTA.

angle.angle: Your enterprise plan is not the hero. It is the anchor.
angle.core_tension: buyers assume cheapest is safest when anchor logic is invisible
angle.why_it_works: It gives a concrete mental model

hooks: If buyers always choose cheapest, your anchor tier is broken
hooks.best_hook: If buyers always choose cheapest, your anchor tier is broken

draft.content: If buyers always choose cheapest, your anchor tier is broken. The top plan should make the middle plan feel safe, not random. Show one premium tier with a clear contrast so the buyer immediately understands why pro is the practical pick. Follow for more SaaS teardown lessons.

tightened.content: If buyers always choose cheapest, your anchor tier is broken. Use the top plan to make the middle option feel safe and obvious. Follow for more SaaS teardown lessons.

checks.item: At most one CTA is present

weakest_parts: Could add one more concrete example
qc.final_script: If buyers always choose cheapest, your anchor tier is broken. Use the top plan to make the middle option feel safe and obvious. Follow for more SaaS teardown lessons.

visual_plan.spoken_line: If buyers always choose cheapest, your anchor tier is broken.
visual_plan.visual: Highlight the cheapest plan selection on a pricing page

pickup_lines_to_capture: The anchor plan exists to frame the middle plan

alternate_hooks: Your top plan is not supposed to sell
